title: Angular 18
description: A staff-level deep dive into Angular 18 signals, the new control flow, deferrable views, change detection internals, and the architectural decisions that separate a senior Angular engineer from the rest.

blocks.text: Angular 18 is the culmination of the framework's multi-year pivot toward fine-grained reactivity. It stabilizes signals as a first-class reactive primitive, ships a built-in template control flow (@if, @for, @switch) that replaces structural directives, introduces deferrable views (@defer) for declarative lazy loading, and delivers experimental zoneless change detection. The standalone component model is now the default, eliminating most NgModule boilerplate.

The mental model shift is significant: instead of relying on Zone.js to monkey-patch every async API and trigger a full top-down change detection sweep, Angular is moving toward a world where state changes notify exactly the views that depend on them. Understanding both worlds — the legacy Zone-based model and the new signal-based one — is what an interviewer is probing for at the senior level.
Angular 18 keeps full backward compatibility: signals interoperate with RxJS via toSignal/toObservable, and you can adopt zoneless incrementally. Nothing forces a rewrite.

For years Angular's biggest performance criticism was Zone.js: any async event (a setTimeout, an XHR, a DOM event anywhere) triggered change detection across the entire component tree. Large apps fought this with OnPush, detached views, and runOutsideAngular hacks. Signals fix the root cause — they create a precise dependency graph so only affected views re-render.

items: Performance: signal-based change detection can skip entire subtrees automatically, and zoneless removes the ~100KB Zone.js polyfill plus its scheduling overhead.
Simpler async: @defer replaces hand-rolled lazy-loading with IntersectionObserver, and the new control flow removes ngIf/ngFor/ngSwitch ceremony.
Smaller bundles: standalone components enable better tree-shaking; removing Zone.js shrinks the initial payload.
Better DX: signals give synchronous, glitch-free reads with no subscription lifecycle to manage, eliminating a whole class of memory leaks from unsubscribed observables.
Future-proofing: the entire framework roadmap (zoneless, resource API, signal forms) is built on signals, so adopting them now is strategic.

blocks.text: Before the new reactivity, you must be fluent in the building blocks. A component pairs a TypeScript class with a template. Directives extend element behavior (attribute directives like ngClass, structural directives historically like *ngIf). Pipes transform values in templates. Services hold reusable logic and are wired in through Angular's hierarchical dependency injection.

Dependency injection in Angular is hierarchical: a token is resolved by walking up from the element injector through component injectors to the root (platform) injector. providedIn: 'root' creates a tree-shakable singleton. Providing a service at the component level creates a new instance scoped to that component and its children — useful for per-feature state.

A signal is a wrapper around a value that notifies consumers when it changes. signal() creates a writable signal; computed() derives a memoized value that recomputes lazily only when a dependency it actually read during evaluation changes; effect() runs a side effect whenever any signal it reads changes.

computed() is lazy and memoized — its body does not run until something reads it, and it caches until a dependency changes. effect() is eager and runs in the injection context; use effects for side effects (logging, DOM, sync to localStorage), never to compute derived state — use computed() for that.

Equality matters: signals use Object.is by default, so setting a signal to a value equal to its current one is a no-op that triggers no recomputation. For object signals you often want a custom equality function or to set a new reference.

Angular 18 replaces the @Input/@Output decorators with signal-based equivalents. input() returns a read-only signal that updates when the parent binding changes. output() creates an emitter with a cleaner API than EventEmitter. model() creates a two-way bindable signal — the foundation for custom form controls and [(value)] syntax.

items: input.required<T>() fails the build if the parent forgets the binding — far safer than runtime undefined checks.
input() supports a transform option, e.g. input(false, { transform: booleanAttribute }) to coerce string attributes.
model() is sugar over an input plus an output named <name>Change, enabling banana-in-a-box [(value)] binding.
Signal inputs eliminate ngOnChanges for most cases — derive with computed() and react with effect().

sections.heading: New Control Flow and Deferrable Views

blocks.text: The built-in control flow is now part of the template syntax instead of structural directives. It is faster (no directive instantiation), type-narrowing aware, and ships without importing CommonModule. @for requires a track expression — this is mandatory and is the single biggest list-rendering performance lever.

@defer lazily loads a block and everything it imports, splitting it into a separate chunk. It supports triggers (on viewport, on idle, on interaction, on hover, on timer) plus @placeholder, @loading, and @error blocks, giving you declarative code-splitting that previously required manual dynamic imports and IntersectionObserver wiring.

track in @for must uniquely identify each item. Using track $index for a list that gets reordered or has items inserted causes Angular to recycle the wrong DOM nodes, leading to lost input state and subtle visual bugs. Track a stable id.

sections.heading: Signals vs RxJS: When to Use Each

blocks.text: Signals and RxJS are complementary, not competitors. Signals model synchronous state — the current value of something, derivations of it, and reactive UI. RxJS models asynchronous event streams over time with rich operators for coordination (debounce, switchMap, retry, combineLatest).

rows: Component/UI state (always has a current value)

blocks.text: Bridge between them with toSignal (Observable to Signal, auto-unsubscribes when the injection context is destroyed) and toObservable (Signal to Observable). A common pattern: take a search input signal, convert to observable, debounce + switchMap the HTTP call, convert the result back to a signal for the template.

sections.heading: Change Detection, Zone.js, OnPush, and Zoneless

blocks.text: Classic Angular uses Zone.js to monkey-patch async APIs (setTimeout, addEventListener, Promise, XHR). When any patched task completes, Zone tells Angular to run change detection, which by default walks the entire component tree top-down comparing template bindings. This is correct but wasteful in large apps.

ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush tells Angular to skip a component (and check it only) when: an @Input reference changes, an event fires inside it, an observable bound via async pipe emits, or you call markForCheck(). With OnPush, components rendering immutable inputs are checked far less often.

Signals are the bridge to zoneless. When a signal read inside a template changes, Angular marks exactly that view dirty without Zone. With provideExperimentalZonelessChangeDetection() you drop Zone.js entirely; change detection is then driven by signal notifications, markForCheck, async pipe, and template event bindings. Code that mutated state inside untracked setTimeout callbacks without a signal will silently stop updating — that is the migration hazard.

OnPush improves performance because Angular's default check is O(all components) per cycle. OnPush converts most components to O(changed inputs), pruning whole subtrees from the dirty-checking pass. Signals take this further: even within a checked component, only the bindings reading a changed signal are updated.

sections.heading: Standalone Architecture, Lazy Loading, Guards, Interceptors

blocks.text: Standalone components declare their own imports and need no NgModule. The app bootstraps with bootstrapApplication and configures providers functionally (provideRouter, provideHttpClient). Lazy loading uses loadComponent or loadChildren returning a dynamic import, and route-level providers scope dependencies to a feature.

items: Functional guards (CanActivateFn) and functional interceptors (HttpInterceptorFn) are the modern, tree-shakable, testable APIs.
loadComponent lazy-loads a single standalone component; loadChildren lazy-loads a route array.
Route-level providers create a child injector, so a lazy feature can have its own scoped services.
withComponentInputBinding() lets route params bind directly to component signal inputs.

sections.heading: Production Example: Signal Store for a Live Cart

blocks.text: Scenario: an e-commerce checkout page where a cart's totals must stay consistent across a header badge, a sidebar summary, and the main list. The team originally used a BehaviorSubject store with async pipes everywhere. Under load, a flurry of rapid add/remove events caused the header total to briefly show stale values — a classic intermediate-emission glitch. They migrated to a signal store.

Because count, subtotal, tax, and total are computed signals, they are always derived from the same snapshot of _lines. The header badge reading count() and the summary reading total() can never observe inconsistent intermediate states — signals are glitch-free. The async-pipe combineLatest version could emit a half-updated state; the signal version cannot. This eliminated the stale-total incident entirely and removed dozens of async pipes.
Note the immutable updates (...spread). Mutating the array in place would not change its reference, so Object.is equality would consider the signal unchanged and no recomputation would fire.

items: Mutating signal values in place (arr.push(x); sig.set(arr)) — same reference means Object.is sees no change and nothing updates. Always set a new reference.
Using computed() for side effects or effect() to set other signals — effects writing to signals are disallowed by default (allowSignalWrites) and indicate you actually wanted computed().
Forgetting track in @for, or using track $index on a reorderable list — leads to recycled DOM nodes, lost focus, and wrong checkbox states.
Calling a signal as a property (count instead of count()) in a template — binds the function itself, not the value. TypeScript may not catch this in string templates.
Migrating to zoneless while still mutating state in raw setTimeout/Promise callbacks without signals — the view stops updating because nothing schedules change detection.
Reading a signal inside an effect conditionally — if the read is skipped on first run, the effect won't re-run when that signal changes later. Dependencies are tracked per execution.
Leaking effects created outside an injection context without a manual destroy — pass a DestroyRef or create them in a constructor/field initializer.

blocks.text: Real incident: a dashboard used effect(() => this.total.set(this.a() + this.b())) to keep a derived total in sync. Under Angular 18 this throws unless allowSignalWrites is set, and even then it created a redundant render pass and an infinite-loop risk. The fix was a one-line computed(). Effects are for the outside world, not for deriving state.

items: Default new components to ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush; with signals it is essentially free correctness and a big perf win.
Model state as signals, derivations as computed, and side effects as effects — never blur these roles.
Expose store state as asReadonly() signals so consumers cannot mutate them directly.
Use signals for state, RxJS for async streams; bridge with toSignal/toObservable rather than reimplementing operators by hand.
Always provide a stable track id in @for; prefer entity ids over $index.
Use @defer with on viewport or prefetch on idle for below-the-fold heavy components instead of manual dynamic imports.
Prefer functional guards and HttpInterceptorFn; they are tree-shakable and trivially unit-testable with TestBed.runInInjectionContext.
Adopt zoneless behind a feature flag in a non-critical route first, audit for raw async state mutations, then roll out.

blocks.text: You do not need a big-bang rewrite. Standalone, signals, new control flow, and zoneless can each be adopted independently. Angular ships schematics: ng generate @angular/core:control-flow migrates *ngIf/*ngFor to @if/@for, and ng generate @angular/core:standalone converts modules. Start with OnPush and signals in leaf components, then work upward.
A pragmatic order: 1) standalone migration, 2) new control flow schematic, 3) convert state to signals component-by-component, 4) flip OnPush on, 5) trial zoneless on an isolated feature.

diagrams.content:
flowchart TD
  A[writable signal count] --> B[computed doubled]
  A --> C[computed isEven]
  B --> D[effect log]
  C --> E[template binding]
  A --> E
  D --> F[console side effect]
  classDef w fill:#cfe,stroke:#093
  classDef c fill:#fec,stroke:#960
  class A w
  class B,C c

diagrams.title: Zoneless Change Detection Sequence
diagrams.content:
sequenceDiagram
  participant U as User
  participant C as Component
  participant S as Signal
  participant CD as ChangeDetector
  U->>C: click increment
  C->>S: count.update
  S->>CD: mark dependent view dirty
  CD->>C: re-evaluate bindings reading count
  C->>U: update DOM only where count is read

diagrams.title: Deferrable View Loading Pipeline
diagrams.content:
+-------------+    on viewport    +--------------+    success    +-------------+
| @placeholder| ----------------> | @loading     | ------------> | rendered    |
| skeleton    |   trigger fires   | spinner      |   chunk ready | component   |
+-------------+                   +--------------+               +-------------+
       |                                 |  error
       | prefetch on idle                v
       +--------------------------> +-----------+
         (chunk fetched early)      | @error    |
                                    | fallback  |
                                    +-----------+

questions.question: What is the relationship between a component and a directive in Angular?
questions.answer: A component is a directive with a template. Internally Angular components are a specialization of directives — they add a view (template + styles). Attribute directives (like ngClass) modify an existing element without their own template, and structural directives historically changed DOM layout. In Angular 18 the structural-directive role for if/for/switch is replaced by built-in control flow, but custom attribute and structural directives still exist.
followUps: When would you still write a custom structural directive?
How do host bindings work on a directive?

questions.question: What is the difference between computed() and effect(), and when do you reach for each?
questions.answer: computed() produces a derived value: it is lazy (its body runs only when read), memoized (cached until a tracked dependency changes), and pure (must not cause side effects). effect() runs side effects: it is eager (runs immediately and re-runs when any signal it reads changes) and is meant for talking to the outside world — logging, DOM manipulation, syncing to storage. Rule of thumb: if you are producing a value, use computed; if you are reacting to a value to do something, use effect. Using effect to set another signal is an anti-pattern that usually means you wanted computed.
followUps: Why are signal writes disallowed in effects by default?
How is computed memoization invalidated?

questions.question: Why does ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush improve performance?
questions.answer: Default change detection checks every component on every cycle (any async event triggers a full top-down dirty check). OnPush changes the condition for checking a component: it is only checked when an input reference changes, an event originates inside it, an async-pipe-bound observable emits, or markForCheck is called. This lets Angular skip entire subtrees that have not been touched, turning O(all components) into roughly O(changed components). Combined with immutable inputs and signals, the checked surface area shrinks dramatically.
followUps: How do signals interact with OnPush?
What can break OnPush silently?

questions.question: When should you use signals versus RxJS in Angular 18?
questions.answer: Use signals for synchronous state that always has a current value: component state, derived values, and template reactivity. Use RxJS for asynchronous streams over time and for coordination operators — debounceTime, switchMap, retry, combineLatest, websocket handling, cancellation. They interoperate: toSignal converts an observable to a signal (auto-unsubscribing), toObservable converts a signal to a stream. A typical pattern keeps user input in a signal, converts it to an observable to debounce and switchMap an HTTP call, then converts the result back to a signal for the template.
followUps: What does toSignal do on destroy?
Why switchMap over mergeMap for search?

questions.question: What does Zone.js do, and what changes when you go zoneless?
questions.answer: Zone.js monkey-patches async browser APIs (setTimeout, Promise, addEventListener, XHR/fetch) so Angular knows when an async task completes and can run change detection automatically. Going zoneless removes that patching: change detection is instead scheduled by signal notifications, the async pipe, markForCheck calls, and template event bindings. The benefit is less overhead and a smaller bundle; the risk is that state mutated inside raw async callbacks without a signal will no longer trigger a UI update because nothing schedules detection.
followUps: How do you bootstrap zoneless?
How do you find code that breaks under zoneless?

questions.question: Explain @defer and its trigger options.
questions.answer: @defer declaratively lazy-loads a template block and everything it imports into a separate JS chunk. Triggers control when the block loads: on idle (requestIdleCallback), on viewport (IntersectionObserver), on interaction, on hover, on timer(ms), or on a custom condition with when. prefetch lets you fetch the chunk ahead of rendering (e.g. prefetch on idle while showing a placeholder). Companion blocks @placeholder, @loading (with after/minimum to avoid flicker), and @error give a full loading UX without manual dynamic import or observer wiring.
followUps: What does minimum on @loading prevent?
Can @defer dependencies be in the eager bundle?

questions.question: What advantages do signal inputs (input()) have over the @Input() decorator?
questions.answer: Signal inputs return read-only signals, so they integrate with computed/effect and trigger fine-grained updates without ngOnChanges. input.required<T>() enforces the binding at compile time, eliminating undefined-input bugs. They support transform functions for coercion (e.g. booleanAttribute, numberAttribute). Reading them is synchronous and reactive, so deriving state from inputs is just a computed instead of an ngOnChanges handler. model() builds on inputs to provide two-way binding.
followUps: How does model() implement two-way binding?
How do you react to an input change?

questions.question: Describe Angular's hierarchical dependency injection and how scoping affects instances.
questions.answer: Angular resolves a token by walking from the element injector up through component injectors to the module/environment injector and finally the platform/root injector. providedIn: 'root' yields a single tree-shakable app-wide instance. Providing a service in a component's providers creates a new instance scoped to that component and its descendants, so each component instance gets its own copy — useful for per-feature stores. Route-level providers create a child environment injector for a lazy feature, scoping its dependencies to that route.
followUps: When does providedIn: 'root' get tree-shaken away?
What is an environment injector vs element injector?

questions.question: TRICKY: If you call counter.set(counter() + 1); counter.set(counter() + 1); twice in a row, what is the final value, and how many times does an effect reading counter run?
questions.answer: The final value is the original + 2 (each set reads the current value synchronously, so the second set sees the first increment). The subtle part is the effect: signal writes are synchronous, but effect scheduling is glitch-free and batched — the effect does not run after each set. It is scheduled once and runs a single time, observing only the final, consistent value. Signals never expose intermediate states to consumers; computed values and effects always see a coherent snapshot. So two sets, value +2, effect runs once. The same applies to computed values, which are recomputed at most once before the next read regardless of how many writes occurred.
followUps: What is a glitch in reactive systems?
When does the effect actually flush?
Does this differ from RxJS BehaviorSubject emitting twice?

questions.question: TRICKY: A computed reads signal A, then conditionally reads signal B only when A is truthy. After A becomes falsy, you change B. Does the computed recompute?
questions.answer: No — and this surprises people. Signal dependencies are tracked dynamically per execution: only the signals actually read during the last computation are dependencies. If on the last run A was falsy so B was never read, B is not a current dependency, so changing B does not invalidate the computed. The dependency set is recomputed each time the body runs. This is correct and efficient, but it means you cannot assume a signal mentioned in the code is always tracked; it is only tracked if the read executed. The same dynamic-dependency rule applies to effects.
followUps: How would you force B to always be a dependency?
What is untracked() for?

questions.question: TRICKY: You call toSignal(http.get(...)) inside a method that is not a constructor or field initializer. What goes wrong?
questions.answer: toSignal must run in an injection context because it registers a teardown (subscription cleanup) with the current DestroyRef. Called outside one — e.g. in a click handler or a plain method invoked later — it throws or, if you pass { manualCleanup: true } or an explicit injector, risks leaking the subscription since there is no DestroyRef to tie it to. The fix is to create it in a field initializer/constructor, or pass an explicit injector via runInInjectionContext or the injector option. The broader gotcha: anything relying on inject() or DestroyRef (effect, toSignal, takeUntilDestroyed) is injection-context sensitive.
followUps: How does runInInjectionContext help?
What does takeUntilDestroyed do?

questions.question: Why is track mandatory in @for, and what breaks if you track $index on a reorderable list?
questions.answer: track tells Angular how to identify each item across renders so it can reuse, move, or destroy the correct DOM nodes instead of recreating the whole list. It is mandatory because the default of recreating everything would be catastrophic for performance. If you track $index on a list that gets reordered or has items inserted/removed in the middle, Angular maps DOM nodes by position, not identity — so it keeps the node at index 0 even though the item there changed, recycling the wrong element. This manifests as wrong checkbox states, lost focus, and stale component state. Track a stable unique id instead.
followUps: When is track $index actually acceptable?
How does track compare to trackBy in *ngFor?

questions.question: How does bootstrapApplication work, and how do you configure app-wide dependencies without an AppModule?
questions.answer: bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, appConfig) starts a standalone app by creating a root environment injector directly from a providers array, so there is no NgModule at all. Instead of importing modules like HttpClientModule or RouterModule.forRoot, you call functional provider factories: provideRouter(routes), provideHttpClient(), provideAnimationsAsync(), and so on. These functions are tree-shakable because unused features are never referenced. For libraries that still ship NgModules, you bridge them with importProvidersFrom(SomeModule) inside the providers array. The convention is to keep the providers in an appConfig: ApplicationConfig object in app.config.ts, which keeps bootstrap clean and testable.
followUps: What does importProvidersFrom do and when do you need it?
How do features like provideRouter stay tree-shakable?

questions.question: What problem does @let solve in Angular templates, and how does it differ from a computed signal?
questions.answer: @let declares a local template variable, letting you name an expression once and reuse it across the template instead of repeating it or leaking a value out of an @if (x; as y) block. For example @let total = subtotal() + tax(); makes total available to sibling elements below it. It is re-evaluated as part of change detection like any other binding, and it is read-only within the template. Unlike a computed signal, @let is not memoized and lives only in the template scope; it is purely a template-authoring convenience, not a reactivity primitive. Reach for computed() when the derivation is expensive or shared across components, and @let for lightweight local naming to improve template readability.
followUps: Can you reassign a @let variable?
What is the scope of an @let declaration?

questions.question: What is non-destructive hydration in Angular SSR, and why is it better than the older approach?
questions.answer: With provideClientHydration(), Angular reuses the server-rendered DOM on the client instead of destroying it and re-rendering from scratch (the legacy behavior that caused a visible flicker and threw away already-painted content). During bootstrap the client walks the existing DOM, attaches event listeners and component state, and only patches differences, which improves Largest Contentful Paint and eliminates layout shift. It also deduplicates HTTP requests made on the server via the transfer-state cache so the client does not re-fetch the same data. Angular 19 adds incremental hydration, driven by @defer (hydrate on ...) triggers, which lets you hydrate parts of the page lazily on interaction or viewport rather than all at once, further reducing initial JavaScript execution.
followUps: What is incremental hydration and which trigger enables it?
How does transfer state avoid duplicate HTTP calls?

questions.question: How do you register functional HttpClient interceptors, and how does interceptor ordering affect a request/response?
questions.answer: You register them with provideHttpClient(withInterceptors([authInterceptor, loggingInterceptor])), where each is an HttpInterceptorFn (req, next) that can inject() dependencies directly. Interceptors form a chain: on the outgoing request they run in array order (auth first, then logging), each calling next(req) to pass control down; on the response they unwind in reverse order. So the last interceptor to touch the request is the first to see the response. Because next returns an Observable, an interceptor can transform, retry, or catchError the response stream. A common mistake is expecting a response transform in the first interceptor to see changes made by a later one, or forgetting that DI-based class interceptors registered via withInterceptorsFromDi() run alongside functional ones in a defined order.
followUps: How do you retry a failed request inside an interceptor?
How do functional and DI interceptors coexist?

questions.question: Compare reactive and template-driven forms, and explain what strictly typed reactive forms give you.
questions.answer: Template-driven forms build the model implicitly from ngModel directives in the template and are simplest for small forms; reactive forms define the model explicitly in the component with FormControl/FormGroup/FormArray, which is more scalable, testable, and easier to compose with dynamic validation. Since Angular 14, reactive forms are strictly typed: FormGroup infers the type of value and valueChanges from its controls, and a FormControl<string | null> reflects that a control can be reset to null. This catches typos in control names and wrong value types at compile time. You opt a control out of nullability with the nonNullable option (or the NonNullableFormBuilder), which makes reset() restore the initial value instead of null. Typed forms turned a large class of runtime this.form.get('naem') bugs into compile-time errors.
followUps: What does nonNullable change about reset()?
When would you still choose template-driven forms?

questions.question: How do you implement a custom synchronous validator, an async validator, and a dynamic FormArray in reactive forms?
questions.answer: A synchronous validator is a ValidatorFn: a function taking an AbstractControl and returning a ValidationErrors object (e.g. { forbidden: true }) or null when valid; you attach it in the control's validators array. An async validator is an AsyncValidatorFn returning a Promise or Observable of ValidationErrors | null, used for server checks like username availability; attach it in the asyncValidators slot, and it runs only after sync validators pass, with the control in 'PENDING' status meanwhile. A FormArray holds a variable-length list of controls: you push()/removeAt() FormGroups to add or remove rows dynamically, and iterate its controls in the template with @for. Cross-field rules (e.g. password match) go on the parent FormGroup as a group-level validator rather than on a single control.
followUps: Why debounce an async username validator?
Where do you put a cross-field validator?

questions.question: How do functional route guards, resolvers, and router input binding work in modern Angular routing?
questions.answer: Guards and resolvers are now plain functions instead of injectable classes. A CanActivateFn is (route, state) => boolean | UrlTree | Observable/Promise of those; it uses inject() to reach services and can return a router.createUrlTree([...]) to redirect. CanMatchFn runs before a route is even matched, so it can hide a lazy chunk entirely (the bundle is not downloaded) rather than merely blocking navigation, which also enables A/B route variants. A ResolveFn<T> pre-fetches data before activation so the component renders with data ready. With provideRouter(routes, withComponentInputBinding()), route params, query params, and resolved data are bound directly to matching component input()s by name, eliminating manual ActivatedRoute subscriptions. These functional APIs are tree-shakable and trivial to unit test with TestBed.runInInjectionContext.
followUps: What is the key difference between CanActivate and CanMatch?
How does withComponentInputBinding map params to inputs?

questions.question: How does content projection with ng-content work, and how do the new signal queries (viewChild/contentChild) differ from the decorator queries?
questions.answer: ng-content projects markup a parent places between a component's tags into the component's template; a bare <ng-content> is the default slot, and multiple <ng-content select="..."> elements create named slots matched by CSS selector, enabling flexible, composable components like cards and dialogs. Projected content is a child of the host in the logical tree, so it is queried with contentChild/ContentChildren, whereas elements the component itself renders are queried with viewChild/ViewChildren. The Angular 17+ signal query functions viewChild()/contentChild() return signals instead of being resolved in ngAfterViewInit/ngAfterContentInit lifecycle hooks; you read them reactively and can derive computeds from them, and viewChild.required() enforces presence at compile-friendly runtime. This removes the timing pitfalls of decorator queries being undefined until the right hook fires.
followUps: When is viewChild() signal populated versus a @ViewChild decorator?
How do you project into multiple named slots?

challenges.title: Debounced Search with Signals and RxJS Interop
challenges.prompt: Build a search component where the user types into an input bound to a signal. Debounce the input by 300ms, cancel stale in-flight requests, call a search API, and expose the results as a signal for the template. Also expose a loading signal. Use toObservable/toSignal to bridge signals and RxJS.
hints: Hold the query in a writable signal updated from the template.
Convert the query signal to an observable with toObservable, then pipe debounceTime and switchMap.
switchMap (not mergeMap) so a new keystroke cancels the previous request.
Wrap the loading flag in the stream (set true on each query, false on result) or derive it, then bring results back with toSignal and an initialValue.
challenges.explanation: The query signal is the synchronous source of truth for what the user typed. toObservable turns its changes into a stream so we can apply time-based operators that signals do not have. debounceTime(300) waits for a pause in typing; distinctUntilChanged avoids re-querying identical text. switchMap is the key choice: it unsubscribes from the previous inner observable when a new query arrives, so a slow earlier request can never overwrite the results of a newer one (no race condition / out-of-order results). startWith emits a loading=true state immediately when a real query begins, and the mapped result emits loading=false with data; catchError keeps the stream alive on API failure. Finally toSignal converts the combined state back into a signal with an initialValue, auto-unsubscribing when the component is destroyed because it runs in the field-initializer injection context. The template reads state() with the new control flow. This is the canonical pattern for combining signal state with RxJS async orchestration.

challenges.title: A Minimal Signal Store with Selectors and Actions
challenges.prompt: Implement a small reusable signal store factory: it takes an initial state object and returns a read-only state signal, a typed select() helper that returns a computed slice, and an update() action that applies an immutable patch. Then build a concrete TodoStore on top of it with computed remaining-count and an addTodo action. Ensure consumers cannot mutate state directly.
hints: Use a private writable signal and expose it via asReadonly().
select() should return computed(() => projector(state())) so slices are memoized.
update() should take a partial or a function and always produce a new object reference.
Build TodoStore by composing the factory; remaining is a computed over the todos slice.
challenges.explanation: The factory encapsulates a private writable signal and only exposes asReadonly(), so no consumer can call set/update on the raw state — mutations must go through the update() action, giving you a single, auditable write path (the same discipline Redux enforces, but with far less boilerplate). select() returns a computed, so each derived slice is memoized and only recomputes when the projected data actually changes; multiple components selecting the same slice share one computed. update() accepts either a partial object or a reducer-style function and always spreads into a new object reference, which is essential: mutating in place would leave the reference unchanged, Object.is would report no change, and nothing would re-render. The TodoStore composes the factory and adds a remaining computed over the todos slice — note remaining recomputes only when todos changes, and because computeds are glitch-free, the UI never sees an inconsistent count mid-update. addTodo and toggle perform immutable array updates (spread and map), preserving references for unchanged items so OnPush children with @for track can skip re-rendering untouched rows. This is essentially a tiny, dependency-free version of NgRx SignalStore.

challenges.title: Infinite Scroll with IntersectionObserver and Signals
challenges.prompt: Build an infinite-scroll list. Maintain items, a page number, and a loading flag as signals. When a sentinel element at the bottom of the list scrolls into view, load the next page and append results. Avoid duplicate loads while a request is in flight, and clean up the observer on destroy.
hints: Keep items, page, loading, and done as signals.
Use a viewChild signal for the sentinel element, or wire IntersectionObserver in ngAfterViewInit.
Guard the load function with the loading and done signals to prevent overlapping requests.
Disconnect the observer in a DestroyRef callback or ngOnDestroy.
challenges.explanation: All mutable state is signals: items (the accumulated list), page (last loaded page), loading (in-flight guard), and done (no more data). viewChild.required returns a signal for the sentinel element. The effect runs once the sentinel is available and creates an IntersectionObserver; crucially it uses the effect's onCleanup callback so the observer is disconnected automatically when the component is destroyed (or if the effect re-runs), preventing a leak — this replaces manual ngOnDestroy bookkeeping. loadNext is guarded by if (this.loading() || this.done()) return, which is the key correctness detail: without it, rapid intersection events or fast scrolling would fire several overlapping requests, appending duplicate pages and corrupting pagination. Setting loading true synchronously before the request closes that window. Items are appended immutably with a spread so the signal gets a new reference and the @for with track item.id reconciles efficiently, reusing existing DOM nodes and only mounting the new rows. When an empty batch returns we set done to stop further loads. The result is a leak-free, race-free infinite scroll built entirely on signals plus one observer.

challenges.title: Typed Reactive Signup Form with Async Username Check and FormArray
challenges.prompt: Build a strictly typed reactive signup form. It has a non-nullable username with an async validator that checks availability against an API (debounced), an email with a built-in email validator, a password and confirmPassword with a group-level cross-field validator ensuring they match, and a FormArray of hobby strings with add/remove. Disable submit while the form is invalid or the async check is pending. Use the new control flow in the template.
hints: Use inject(NonNullableFormBuilder) so controls are non-nullable and reset() restores initial values.
The async validator returns Observable<ValidationErrors | null>; debounce and switchMap the API call, mapping taken usernames to { taken: true }.
Put the password-match validator on the FormGroup, not on a single control, and read it as form.errors.
Model hobbies as a FormArray<FormControl<string>> and push/removeAt to mutate it; iterate with @for over controls.
challenges.explanation: NonNullableFormBuilder makes every control's value type exclude null, so form.getRawValue() is fully typed with concrete strings and arrays and reset() restores the initial values instead of null, which is what a signup form actually wants. The async username validator returns an Observable and is placed in the asyncValidators slot, so it runs only after the synchronous required/minLength checks pass; timer(300) debounces keystrokes and switchMap cancels a stale in-flight availability check when the user keeps typing, mapping a taken username to a { taken: true } error and swallowing network errors so a failed check never blocks the user. While it runs the control is in PENDING status, which is why the template shows a checking indicator and the submit button reads form.pending. The password-match rule is a group-level validator attached to the FormGroup rather than a single control, because it depends on two sibling values; its error surfaces as form.hasError('mismatch'). Hobbies are a strongly typed FormArray whose controls are pushed and removed dynamically and rendered with @for using track $index (acceptable here because the inputs are positional and edited in place, not reordered by identity). The submit button binds [disabled] to form.invalid || form.pending so the user cannot submit while validation is unresolved, and getRawValue is used so disabled controls (if any) are still included in the typed payload.

challenges.title: Lazy Feature Routing with CanMatch, a Resolver, and Router Input Binding
challenges.prompt: Configure routing for an app with a lazily loaded 'reports' feature. Only load the reports chunk when a feature flag is enabled using a functional CanMatchFn (so the bundle is never downloaded otherwise, and an alternate route can take over). Add a report detail route that resolves the report data before activation with a ResolveFn, and bind the route :id param and the resolved data directly to the component's signal inputs. Show the appConfig wiring and the detail component.
hints: Use CanMatchFn (not CanActivateFn) so a disabled feature skips the route entirely and falls through to the next match.
Enable param-to-input binding with provideRouter(routes, withComponentInputBinding()).
A ResolveFn<Report> injects the API and returns the data (Observable is fine) so the component gets it ready on load.
Give the resolved value a route data key and an input() of the same name; the :id path param binds to an input() named id.
challenges.explanation: The feature flag is enforced with a CanMatchFn rather than a CanActivateFn, and that distinction is the whole point: CanMatch runs during route matching, so when it returns false Angular does not even download the lazy reports chunk and instead falls through to the next route with the same path, letting a lightweight 'disabled' component take over. A CanActivateFn would have downloaded the bundle and then blocked navigation, wasting bytes and losing the fallthrough behavior. The ResolveFn injects the ReportApi with inject() and returns the report (an Observable is unwrapped by the router) so the detail component never has to render a loading state for its primary data or subscribe manually. withComponentInputBinding() is the key wiring that lets the router bind the :id path parameter and the resolve data keyed 'report' straight into input()s of the same names, so ReportDetailComponent needs no ActivatedRoute subscription at all and stays trivially unit-testable. Because the inputs are signals, derived values like heading are just computeds. The functional guard and resolver are tree-shakable and, since they are plain functions using inject(), they are simple to test with TestBed.runInInjectionContext. This is the canonical modern-routing shape: lazy loading gated by CanMatch, data pre-fetched by a ResolveFn, and everything delivered to the component through signal input binding.
